author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: KLI Tutorial - Heartnet  Love Letter Ever needed to send a love letter and trust that your recipient knew it was really you that sent it? If so then this post is for you! KERI provides the end-verifiability and secure attribution you need to trust your communications are secure. Join Brett and Allie as they show how to send a secure love letter and a reply using KERI. Follow along with the companion video of their story: KERI KLI Series: Sign and Verify. 7/24/24 Update: Uses latest development version of KERI v1.2.0-dev10. Added new Git repository https://github.com/kentbull/allie-brett and added both heartnet-cli-workflow.sh and heartnet-docker-workflow.sh scripts for each workflow, Command Line and Docker. Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC).

author: Kent Bull
content: I make frequent use of the shell continuation character \ to provide readability to commands. This could be hard on the eyes of seasoned professionals though it is helpful for those new to KERI and those who appreciate separate arguments on separate lines. You do not need to use this continuation character when you use the commands. You can omit it and type everything on one line.

content: If you are going to install with OS X then be sure to translate all of the "/keri" directories to whatever the root directory is you are using for this tutorial work. I will use $HOME/allie-brett. You will also need to translate the python commands to whatever you end up having on your system whether python3, python3.10, or anything like that. I use python because I set up my system to point to my Pyenv installation in $HOME/.pyenv/shims/python binary.

content:
Libsodium: First, install download and build the Microsoft vcpkg tool at the Powershell command line: mkdir vcpkg
cd vcpkg
git clone https://github.com/Microsoft/vcpkg.git .
./bootstrap-vcpkg.bat

                
            POWERSHELL Next use the vcpkg tool to install libsodium on your computer. At the Powershell command line: ./vcpkg integrate install
./vcpkg install libsodium

                
            POWERSHELL Alternatively, you can follow the directions for pre-built libraries at: https://doc.libsodium.org/installation/ There is a possibility the above may silently fail to place libsodium.dll in the Windows\System32 directory. You may need to manually copy the DLL from the pre-built library download into your Windows\System32 directory. As of this writing, the most recent version of the DLL is located in the pre-built library zip file at: \libsodium-1.0.18-msvc\libsodium\x64\Release\v142\dynamic\libsodium.dll

Verify your installation $ kli version

                
            POWERSHELL This should output something like 0.6.8, 0.6.9, or 1.0.0. This fails on Windows with the error: AttributeError: module 'socket' has no attribute 'AF_UNIX' This is due to AF_UNIX port types not being supported by Windows. Interestingly, the code is actually trying to open a logging file, which should be done a different way. It could be said that the flaw exists in the code. In order to fix this, the following code modifications were necessary the ioflo/hio library, specifically in the ogling.py file. ioflo/hio is the async runtime KERI is built on top of.

if sys.platform == 'win32':
  # cur_filename is obtained from the os.path so it matches the source code file.
  # a better fix would be using the app name or something else relevant to the logger.
  cur_filename = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(__file__))[0]
  log_filename = 'logs' + os.sep + 'Log_' + cur_filename + '.txt'

  # make sure the log directory exists
  if not os.path.exists("logs"):
    os.makedirs('logs')

  # use a logging handler more appropriate to windows
  self.baseSysLogHandler = logging.handlers.WatchedFileHandler(
    os.environ.get("LOGFILE", log_filename))
else:
  # if not on Windows, use the old way
  facility = logging.handlers.SysLogHandler.LOG_USER # LOG_DAEMON
  self.baseSysLogHandler = logging.handlers.SysLogHandler(address=address, 
                                                          facility=facility)

content: Step 2: Start Witness Network
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Starting a witness network takes a few commands in the provided script. You can also just start the demo witnesses in another terminal like below. The reason why you need a witness network is to provide duplicity detection for key events in key event logs, though that is overkill for this tutorial. This tutorial shows you how to use witnesses to give you a gentle, light introduction to them. Witnesses are not strictly needed for signing and verification to work.

author: Kent Bull
content: The script uses a few more commands including wait_for, which is a handy utility to wait for connections on a port to be accepted in order to move on. After starting the witnesses in the background with the trailing ampersand the process identifier (pid) is retrieved from the preceding command with $! and added to the PID_LIST variable that is used in the kill -9 $PID_LIST command towards the end of the script.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content:
wait_for() {
  local host=$1
  local port=$2
  local timeout=${3:-30}

  for ((i=0; i<timeout; i++)); do
    if nc -z "$host" "$port"; then
      return 0
    fi
    sleep 1
  done

  return 1
}

#
# Start witness network
#
PID_LIST=""
kli witness demo &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+="${pid}"
print_green "Witness network started with PID: ${pid}"
print_lcyan "Waiting 5 seconds for witness network to accept connections"
sleep 5

author: Kent Bull
content:
wait_for() {
  local host=$1
  local port=$2
  local timeout=${3:-30}

  for ((i=0; i<timeout; i++)); do
    if nc -z "$host" "$port"; then
      return 0
    fi
    sleep 1
  done

  return 1
}

#
# Start witness network
#
PID_LIST=""
kli witness demo &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+="${pid}"
print_green "Witness network started with PID: ${pid}"
print_lcyan "Waiting 5 seconds for witness network to accept connections"
sleep 5
# Wait for the HTTP port 5642 or TCP port 5632 to be accepting connections
if wait_for 127.0.0.1 5642 || wait_for 127.0.0.1 5632; then
  print_green "Witness network is accepting connections"
else
  print_red "Timeout waiting for witness network to accept connections"
  exit 1
fi

author: Kent Bull
content: Step 5: Create KERI identifiers by making an inception event
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: The most important part to being able to use KERI is the creation of KERI identifiers, also known as Autonomic Identifiers, or AIDs. One identifier will be created for each Allie and Brett. The witnesses in the witness pools will each observe, or witness, the inception event and give a receipt of that observation back to the AID controller.

# Create inception configuration files for Allie and Brett
read -r -d '' ALLIE_AID_INCEPTION_CONFIG <<EOM

kli incept \

  "transferable": true,
  "wits": [
    "${WIT_WITNESS_AID}",
    "${WUB_WITNESS_AID}",
    "${WYZ_WITNESS_AID}"
  ],
  "toad": 3,
  "icount": 1,
  "ncount": 1,
  "isith": "1",
  "nsith": "1"
}
EOM

temp_brett_aid_inception_config=$(mktemp)
echo "${BRETT_AID_INCEPTION_CONFIG}" >${temp_brett_aid_inception_config}
cp -v ${temp_brett_aid_inception_config} ${temp_brett_aid_inception_config}.json
print_lcyan "Brett's inception configuration file created"
cat ${temp_brett_aid_inception_config}.json
echo

# Create inception event for Brett
print_green "Creating inception event for Brett"
kli incept \
    --name brett_ks \
    --alias secret-speaker \
    -f ${temp_brett_aid_inception_config}.json

author: Kent Bull

author: Kent Bull

author: Kent Bull
content: Step 7: Increase the trust level with MFA challenge phrases
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Purpose of KERI Challenge Response and Verification – Authenticity
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Brett and Allie need to know they are really talking to each other over the internet. They need authenticity of their digital communications, meaning they both know exactly who they are talking to over the internet. Since packets can be forged, man in the middle attacks (MITM) can be performed, and other attacks like eclipse attacks, then people need a way to ensure authenticity of communications. The challenge response and verification process provide that authenticity.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Purpose of KERI Challenge Response and Verification – Authenticity

author: Kent Bull
content: For Allie and Brett they both generate their own challenge phrase which they send to the other party using an out of band communication channel. What out of band means in this context is something that is out of band with respect to the internet infrastructure transmitting text across the internet. Using a video call and the on-call chat box or a voice call is sufficient. The point is to not use the same communication channel to send the challenge words that is used to send the challenge response because you are trying to protect from man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks that target internet infrastructure. If you don’t use out of band communication to send the challenge words then you don’t know for sure whether or not you are suffering from a MITM attack.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Purpose of KERI Challenge Response and Verification – Authenticity

author: Kent Bull
content: So to repeat out of band in this context means using two different communications channels to send different parts of the challenge response message. Using a on-call chat for something like a Zoom call, even though it is across the internet, is considered sufficiently out of band because you can be reasonably assured that hackers have not hacked Zoom’s infrastructure to change your challenge response words. If you have need for the absolute highest levels of security then you can transmit challenge words using voice or other means such as a physical piece of paper, USB drive, or other secure transport.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Purpose of KERI Challenge Response and Verification – Authenticity

author: Kent Bull
content: Performing KERI Challenge Response and Verification
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: To perform the challenge response and verification process between two identifiers each party can send a challenge to the other identifier using the kli challenge generate/respond/verify commands that if successfully met confirms that each party truly controls their identifier.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Performing KERI Challenge Response and Verification

author: Kent Bull
content: What this breaks down into is
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: First Brett sets up the challenge to verify the identifier Allie is using is really the one he expects her to be using. He will create a set of challenge words, have her sign the words, send back a message with the words and a signature over those words, and then verify that signature against Allie’s public key as found in her key event log (KEL).

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Like Brett, Allie prepares a set of challenge words that she will deliver to Brett in an out of band communication channel such as a video or voice call.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content:
export ALLIE_WORDS="$(kli challenge generate --out string)"

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: These are the words that Allie will give to Brett to verify she is indeed communicating with Brett’s identifier.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Brett then similarly prepares a response to Allie’s challenge words and Allie verifies Brett’s challenge response.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Brett preparing a response to Allie’s challenge words:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli challenge respond \
    --name brett_ks \
    --alias secret-speaker \
    --recipient magic-pencil \
    --words "${ALLIE_WORDS}"

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli challenge respond \
    --name brett_ks \
    --alias secret-speaker \
    --recipient magic-pencil \
    --words "${ALLIE_WORDS}"
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Allie verifying Brett’s challenge response:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content:
$ kli challenge verify \
      --name allie_ks \
      --base /keri/allie \
      --alias magic-pencil \
      --signer secret-speaker \
      --words "${ALLIE_WORDS}"

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

      --name allie_ks \

author: Kent Bull
content: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Now we can finally do what we came here to do! It’s time to write the letter and sign it.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content: Allie writes the first love letter using a temporary file that she separately sends to Brett.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
love_letter=$(mktemp)
cp -v ${love_letter} ${love_letter}.json
echo '{"love_letter": "well, hello there, honey. Happy Valentines :*"}' >${love-letter}.json

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
love_letter=$(mktemp)
cp -v ${love_letter} ${love_letter}.json
echo '{"love_letter": "well, hello there, honey. Happy Valentines :*"}' >${love-letter}.json
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: And Allie then signs it:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli sign \
    --name allie_ks \
    --alias magic-pencil \
    --text ${love_letter}.json

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Since the output includes a numbered list then we have to parse that list to get just the bare signature.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content: Output (is a signature):
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
1. AAAZP-KPS1FtkeVNvvm8uSFJqlDNmJy8tpD1hB2-OYcmlP39Fog4wETcDEL_4QNXHeUVV_QiuUpqbxgIzAaiV9EK

DEFAULT
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content: 1. AAAZP-KPS1FtkeVNvvm8uSFJqlDNmJy8tpD1hB2-OYcmlP39Fog4wETcDEL_4QNXHeUVV_QiuUpqbxgIzAaiV9EK
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: To parse this and place it in a variable the script uses a combination command using sed and command substitution:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
export ALLIE_SIGNATURE=$(kli sign \
    --name allie_ks \
    --alias magic-pencil \
    --text ${love_letter}.json | sed -E 's/^[0-9]+\. //')

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 8: Write and sign the love letter

author: Kent Bull
content:
export ALLIE_SIGNATURE=$(kli sign \
    --name allie_ks \
    --alias magic-pencil \
    --text ${love_letter}.json | sed -E 's/^[0-9]+\. //')
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull

content: Step 9: Verify the love letter signature
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet

author: Kent Bull
content: Brett wants to know this letter really is from Allie so he verifies it with kli verify. Brett verifies Allie’s signature on the love letter:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Step 9: Verify the love letter signature

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli verify \
    --name brett_ks \
    --alias secret-speaker \
    --prefix ${ALLIE_PREFIX} \
    --text ${love_letter}.json \
    --signature $ALLIE_SIGNATURE

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series – KLI: Sign and Verify with Heartnet
